title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Project goals
This book documents the migration of NOAA CoastWatch West Coast Node / SWFSC ERDDAP services and associated data processing workflows to Google Cloud Platform (GCP). The documentation is intended to serve as a practical technical record of the cloud architecture, serving environment, reusable deployment patterns, and operational workflows developed during the migration.
The project is part of a broader NOAA/NMFS effort to move data systems away from legacy on-premises infrastructure and into cloud-based environments. The goal is not only to preserve existing ERDDAP services and operational data products, but also to make the supporting workflows more reproducible, maintainable, and easier to operate over time.
This documentation tracks the design, migration, and modernization of data systems used to support operational ocean data access.
The main goals are to:

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
migrate ERDDAP-supporting workflows from legacy servers to GCP
preserve scientific continuity with existing data products and access patterns
standardize workflow structure across projects
reduce hard-coded paths, credentials, and hidden system assumptions
support reproducible infrastructure and containerized processing
document operational details for review, handoff, maintenance, and future workflow development

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Cloud architecture
The migration uses a combination of long-lived serving infrastructure, processing environments, and scheduled cloud-native jobs.
At the center of the system is a dedicated ERDDAP VM configured for high-performance data serving. The ERDDAP environment uses persistent storage for caches and working directories, rclone-mounted Google Cloud Storage buckets for dataset access, and a Tomcat-hosted ERDDAP instance for data distribution.
Operational processing is handled through two main patterns. Processing VMs support interactive development, testing, validation, reprocessing, and heavier data-generation tasks. Cloud Run Jobs support containerized workflows that run on a schedule or on demand. Cloud Scheduler triggers recurring jobs, Artifact Registry stores workflow container images, and Secret Manager provides credentials and runtime secrets without storing them directly in code.
Across the system, reusable templates and consistent repository structures are used to make workflows easier to deploy, compare, and maintain.

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Workflow modernization
Many of the workflows documented here began as long-running operational scripts developed over many years. As part of the migration, these workflows have been reorganized into more standardized, reproducible, and cloud-ready systems.
This modernization includes separating configuration from runtime logic, reducing machine-specific assumptions, containerizing processing code, standardizing deployment scripts, and documenting input/output paths, scheduling behavior, and ERDDAP integration. The intent is to preserve the underlying science and operational products while improving maintainability and transparency.
For the shared project structures and deployment patterns used across workflows, see the GCP Data Processing Templates chapter.

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Project sections
This book is organized into three main sections.

Cloud setup
The cloud setup chapters document the shared infrastructure, VM access patterns, ERDDAP serving environment, processing VM setup, migration/recovery notes, and reusable deployment templates.

Setup Overview provides a high-level map of the setup documentation.
Cloud Infrastructure Setup documents Terraform-managed VMs, buckets, disks, IAM, and snapshot policies.
Accessing VMs documents SSH, IAP tunneling, browser SSH, and port forwarding.
ERDDAP VM Setup documents the dedicated ERDDAP serving environment.
Processing VM Setup documents processing and development VM configuration.
Migration, Upgrade, and Recovery documents snapshot and cleanup steps used during rebuilds or migrations.
GCP Data Processing Templates describes the Cloud Run and processing VM template repositories used to standardize workflow structure across projects.



Workflows
The workflow chapters document individual operational pipelines migrated or adapted for cloud-based processing.

MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor documents the Cloud Run workflow for retrieving and publishing MODIS-Aqua MH1 ocean color, MH1 SST, MPIC, and MPOC products.
VIIRS Net Primary Productivity documents the daily and monthly VIIRS NetPP workflow for SNPP and NOAA-20.
MUR SST documents the MUR SST, anomaly, frontal, and monthly-processing workflows.
C-HARM documents the containerized C-HARM processing workflow.



Presentations
The presentations section collects project update slides and status material used during the migration.

Presentations and Project Updates provides access to the ERDDAP cloud migration presentation materials.

title: SWFSC ERDDAP Cloud Migration
text:
Status
This documentation is a living technical record. It is intended to capture both the final cloud architecture and the practical operational details needed to maintain, troubleshoot, and extend the migrated workflows.

title: Setup Overview
text:
This section documents the Google Cloud Platform (GCP) infrastructure and system setup used to support the SWFSC CoastWatch West Coast Node cloud environment.
The setup documentation is split into smaller pages so each phase of the migration can be read independently. Together, these pages cover Terraform-managed infrastructure, VM access, the ERDDAP serving environment, processing VM configuration, migration/recovery notes, and reusable workflow deployment templates.


  
    Cloud Infrastructure Setup
    Terraform-managed VMs, buckets, service accounts, IAM bindings, disks, labels, and snapshot policies.
    
      Open cloud infrastructure setup
    
  

  
    Accessing VMs
    Connecting to ERDDAP and processing VMs with gcloud SSH, IAP tunneling, browser SSH, and local port forwarding.
    
      Open VM access guide
    
  

  
    ERDDAP VM Setup
    Operating-system setup, persistent disk initialization, rclone-mounted GCS access, Tomcat, and ERDDAP installation.
    
      Open ERDDAP VM setup
    
  

  
    Processing VM Setup
    Processing VM setup for development, testing, reprocessing, Python environments, Earthdata access, and external tools.
    
      Open processing VM setup
    
  

  
    Migration, Upgrade, and Recovery
    Optional data-preservation snapshots, rebuild notes, upgrade workflows, and cleanup steps used during cloud migration work.
    
      Open migration and recovery notes
    
  

  
    GCP Data Processing Templates
    Reusable Cloud Run and processing VM project structures used to standardize workflow repositories and deployments.
    
      Open processing templates

title: Cloud Infrastructure Setup
Accessing VMs
text:
Once infrastructure is deployed, you can connect to any VM using either the gcloud CLI or the GCP Console browser-based SSH. Both methods use Identity-Aware Proxy (IAP) tunneling, so no public IP address or open firewall ports are required.

Prerequisites
Before connecting to a VM for the first time, complete the following setup steps.

1. Install the Google Cloud SDK
Download and install the Google Cloud SDK for your operating system. The SDK provides the gcloud command-line tool used throughout this documentation.
After installation, initialize the SDK:
gcloud init
This walks you through selecting a Google account and default project.


2. Authenticate
Log in with your Google account:
gcloud auth login
This opens a browser window to complete authentication. Once done, confirm the active account and project:
gcloud config list


3. Confirm IAP Access
Connecting to VMs through the CLI requires the IAP-secured Tunnel User (roles/iap.tunnelResourceAccessor) IAM role on the project. In this setup, that role is managed by IT through a centralized roles.tf Terraform configuration — you will need to request access from whoever administers that if you don’t have it yet.
Once access has been granted, confirm the role is assigned to your account:
gcloud projects get-iam-policy YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --flatten="bindings[].members" \
    --filter="bindings.members:YOUR_EMAIL@noaa.gov" \
    --format="table(bindings.role)"
roles/iap.tunnelResourceAccessor should appear in the output. If it does not, follow up with your IT team or project admin.



Via the gcloud CLI
The general pattern for connecting to a VM is:
gcloud compute ssh YOUR_VM_NAME \
    --project=YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --zone=YOUR_GCP_ZONE \
    --tunnel-through-iap
Port forwarding is added via --ssh-flag to make local services on the VM accessible in your browser. The -4 flag forces IPv4, which avoids common IAP tunneling issues.
ERDDAP VM — forwards both the ERDDAP web interface (port 8080) and JupyterLab (port 8888):
gcloud compute ssh YOUR_ERDDAP_VM_NAME \
    --project=YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --zone=YOUR_GCP_ZONE \
    --tunnel-through-iap \
    --ssh-flag="-4 -L 8080:localhost:8080 -L 8888:localhost:8888"
Once connected, the ERDDAP interface is accessible at http://localhost:8080/erddap in your local browser (ERDDAP runs continuously via Tomcat). To use JupyterLab, start it on the VM first:
jupyter lab --no-browser --port=8888
Jupyter will print a URL with a token to the terminal — copy and paste the http://localhost:8888/lab?token=... URL into your local browser.
Dataproc / processing VM — forwards JupyterLab (port 8081 to avoid conflicts if the ERDDAP VM tunnel is already open):
gcloud compute ssh YOUR_DATAPROC_VM_NAME \
    --project=YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --zone=YOUR_GCP_ZONE \
    --tunnel-through-iap \
    --ssh-flag="-4 -L 8081:localhost:8081"
Once connected, start JupyterLab on the VM:
jupyter lab --no-browser --port=8081
Jupyter will print a URL with a token to the terminal — copy and paste the http://localhost:8081/lab?token=... URL into your local browser.


Via the GCP Console
For quick access without the CLI:

Go to the Compute Engine VM instances page in the GCP Console.
Find your VM in the list.
Click the SSH button in the Connect column.

The console opens a browser-based terminal connected to the VM through IAP. This is convenient for quick checks but does not support port forwarding or persistent sessions.

title: ERDDAP VM Setup
text:
This section describes the initialization and configuration of the dedicated ERDDAP serving environment after infrastructure has been deployed. The ERDDAP VM uses C4 high-memory instances (for example, c4-highmem-16) together with Hyperdisk Balanced storage, rclone-mounted GCS buckets for dataset access, and Tomcat-hosted ERDDAP for data distribution.

1. Configure the Operating System Environment

1.1 Configure Shared User Group Permissions
Create a shared group for operational data access and add the required users:
sudo groupadd cwatch                              # Create the cwatch group if it does not already exist
sudo usermod -aG cwatch $USER                     # Add your current user
sudo usermod -aG cwatch YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME # Add the operational/service account (e.g. erd)
newgrp cwatch                                     # Refresh group membership (or log out and back in)


1.2 Initialize and Mount the Persistent Data Disk
Before formatting or mounting the persistent data disk, determine whether the disk is new or reattached from an existing deployment. If the disk was attached from a previous VM, do not reformat it.
# Identify the attached disk
ls -l /dev/disk/by-id/

# Check whether the disk already contains a filesystem
sudo blkid /dev/disk/by-id/google-erddap-data

# If no filesystem exists, create one
sudo mkfs.ext4 -m 0 -E lazy_itable_init=0,lazy_journal_init=0,discard \
    /dev/disk/by-id/google-erddap-data

# Create mount point and mount the disk
sudo mkdir -p /mnt/disks/erddap-data
sudo mount -o discard,defaults \
    /dev/disk/by-id/google-erddap-data \
    /mnt/disks/erddap-data

# Set permissions for the shared cwatch group
sudo chown -R root:cwatch /mnt/disks/erddap-data
sudo chmod -R 2775 /mnt/disks/erddap-data

# Optional: standard symlink used by operational workflows
sudo ln -s /mnt/disks/erddap-data /data


1.3 Configure GCS Connectivity with Rclone
Rclone is used to mount GCS buckets directly into the ERDDAP VM filesystem using a Virtual File System (VFS) mount. The persistent high-speed data disk acts as a local VFS cache to improve ERDDAP performance when serving large NetCDF datasets from cloud storage.
Install rclone:
sudo curl https://rclone.org/install.sh | sudo bash
Create a shared rclone configuration:
sudo mkdir -p /home/YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME/rclone/

sudo tee /home/YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME/rclone/rclone.conf > /dev/null <<EOF
[gcs-remote]
type = google cloud storage
env_auth = true
project_number = YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_NUMBER
location = YOUR_GCP_REGION
EOF

sudo chown -R root:cwatch /home/YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME/rclone/
Create the local cache and mount directories, then create the systemd persistence service:
sudo tee /etc/systemd/system/rclone-mount.service > /dev/null <<EOF
[Unit]
Description=Rclone High-Performance GCS Mount
After=network-online.target mnt-disks-erddap\x2ddata.mount
Wants=network-online.target

[Service]
Type=notify
User=root

ExecStart=/usr/bin/rclone mount gcs-remote:YOUR_ERDDAP_BUCKET_NAME /mnt/gcs/erd \
    --config /home/YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME/rclone/rclone.conf \
    --allow-other \
    --read-only \
    --dir-cache-time 1h \
    --attr-timeout 1m \
    --vfs-cache-mode full \
    --vfs-cache-max-age 48h \
    --vfs-cache-max-size 2000G \
    --vfs-cache-poll-interval 1m \
    --cache-dir /mnt/disks/erddap-data/rclone-cache \
    --buffer-size 128M \
    --vfs-read-ahead 256M \
    --vfs-read-chunk-size 64M \
    --vfs-read-chunk-size-limit 2G \
    --dir-perms 0775 \
    --file-perms 0775 \
    --gid $(getent group cwatch | cut -d: -f3) \
    --umask 002 \
    --rc \
    --rc-no-auth

ExecStop=/usr/bin/fusermount -u /mnt/gcs/erd
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=10

[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target
EOF
Key rclone mount notes:

--read-only prevents accidental modification of cloud-hosted datasets.
--vfs-cache-mode full enables local caching on the persistent disk.
--vfs-read-ahead improves sequential read performance for ERDDAP requests.
--attr-timeout 1m helps rclone detect when NRT files are replaced by science-quality versions in GCS.

Enable and start the service:
sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable rclone-mount.service
sudo systemctl start rclone-mount.service
Verify the mount and cache status:
ls -l /mnt/gcs/erd       # Confirm GCS bucket contents are visible
rclone rc vfs/stats       # Monitor cache usage and read-ahead status






Tip



Storage capacity: The 2 TB cache is a managed ceiling — rclone will automatically purge the oldest data if it hits this limit, ensuring the disk never fills up.
NRT updates: By checking attributes once per minute, the system will automatically fetch the updated science-quality version of a file even if the filename has not changed.





2. Install and Configure ERDDAP

2.1 Run the CoastWatch ERDDAP Installation Script
This setup uses the CoastWatch ERDDAP installation script to install Tomcat, ERDDAP, Java dependencies, and supporting system configuration.
wget https://raw.githubusercontent.com/erd-coastwatch/cloud-erddap-setup/main/setup_scripts/erddap_setup.sh
chmod +x erddap_setup.sh
./erddap_setup.sh

# If reinstalling, remove the previous Tomcat installation first
sudo rm -rf /usr/local/apache-tomcat-10.1.44/
./erddap_setup.sh


2.2 Configure Tomcat and ERDDAP
sudo su -s /bin/bash tomcat
cd /usr/local/tomcat_erddap/conf
vi server.xml

cd /usr/local/tomcat_erddap/content/erddap
vi setup.xml


2.3 Start and Verify ERDDAP
# Start ERDDAP
sudo su -s /bin/bash tomcat
cd /usr/local/tomcat_erddap/bin
./startup.sh

# Stop ERDDAP
./shutdown.sh

# Verify installation
curl http://localhost:8080

# Install additional dependencies
sudo apt install -y fontconfig
sudo apt install -y lynx

title: Processing VM Setup
text:
This section describes the initialization and configuration of processing environments used for operational workflows, testing, reprocessing, and data generation. Unlike the ERDDAP VM, which is optimized for data serving, processing VMs are intended for workflow execution, algorithm development, backfills, and validation tasks.
Processing environments typically use C4 standard instances (for example, c4-standard-16) together with Hyperdisk Balanced storage, prioritizing compute performance over the additional memory used by ERDDAP serving environments.

1. Configure the Operating System Environment

1.1 Configure Shared User Group Permissions
sudo groupadd cwatch                              # Create the cwatch group if it does not already exist
sudo usermod -aG cwatch $USER                     # Add your current user
sudo usermod -aG cwatch YOUR_OPERATIONAL_USERNAME # Add the operational/service account (e.g. erd)
newgrp cwatch                                     # Refresh group membership (or log out and back in)


1.2 Initialize and Mount Persistent Storage
ls -l /dev/disk/by-id/
sudo blkid /dev/disk/by-id/google-processing-data

sudo mkfs.ext4 -m 0 -E lazy_itable_init=0,lazy_journal_init=0,discard \
    /dev/disk/by-id/google-processing-data

sudo mkdir -p /mnt/disks/processing-data
sudo mount -o discard,defaults \
    /dev/disk/by-id/google-processing-data \
    /mnt/disks/processing-data

sudo chown -R root:cwatch /mnt/disks/processing-data
sudo chmod -R 2775 /mnt/disks/processing-data
sudo ln -s /mnt/disks/processing-data /data



2. Configure the Data Processing Environment

2.1 Install Conda Package Manager
curl -O https://repo.anaconda.com/miniconda/Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
bash Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh
conda config --add channels conda-forge

# Create and activate a project environment
conda create --name ENV_NAME
conda activate ENV_NAME

# Install common scientific and workflow dependencies
conda install numpy pandas netCDF4 python-dateutil
conda install -c conda-forge nco
conda install -c conda-forge podaac-data-subscriber

# Clean up
rm Miniconda3-latest-Linux-x86_64.sh


2.2 Configure NASA Earthdata Authentication
Many satellite workflows require NASA Earthdata authentication. Create a .netrc file in the user home directory:
echo "machine urs.earthdata.nasa.gov login YOUR_EARTHDATA_USERNAME password YOUR_EARTHDATA_PASSWORD" >> ~/.netrc
chmod 600 ~/.netrc






Warning



Do not commit .netrc, cookies, passwords, or credentials to Git repositories.




2.3 Test External Data Access
This example uses MUR4.2 as a test dataset — the files are small and the workflow validates NASA authentication, external data access, local storage, and the processing environment.
mkdir -p /data/podaac/data

podaac-data-downloader \
    -c MUR25-JPL-L4-GLOB-v04.2 \
    -d /data/podaac/data \
    --start-date 2025-08-01T21:00:00Z \
    --end-date 2025-08-10T21:00:00Z


2.4 Install External Processing Software
Some workflows require additional compiled tools. Two common examples:
DINEOF — for gap-filling satellite data:
sudo apt-get update
sudo apt-get install -y gfortran make libarpack2-dev libnetcdf-dev libnetcdff-dev git

git clone https://github.com/Aida-Alvera/DINEOF
cd DINEOF/
cp config.mk.template config.mk
make
CoastWatch Utilities (cwutils) — download the appropriate Linux package from the CoastWatch Utilities distribution site and transfer it to the processing VM:
# Transfer from local machine
gcloud compute scp \
    /path/to/cwutils-4_1_0_397-linux-x86_64.tar.gz \
    YOUR_USERNAME@YOUR_PROCESSING_VM_NAME:~/

# Unpack on the VM
tar -zxf cwutils-4_1_0_397-linux-x86_64.tar.gz
rm cwutils-4_1_0_397-linux-x86_64.tar.gz



3. Configure Project Workspace
Processing VMs maintain one directory per workflow or project using a standardized template that provides a consistent structure across workflows. See the GCP Data Processing Templates page for the full template reference.
Project template repository: erd-coastwatch/vm-dataproc-template
cd ~
git clone https://github.com/erd-coastwatch/vm-dataproc-template.git
cp -R vm-dataproc-template YOUR_PROJECT_NAME   # Example: viirs_netpp
cd YOUR_PROJECT_NAME
Create a persistent storage directory for the project and replace the template data directory with a symlink:
mkdir -p /data/projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NAME
rm -rf data
ln -s /data/projects/YOUR_PROJECT_NAME data
This keeps large datasets, temporary products, downloads, and workflow outputs separated from the VM boot disk while maintaining a lightweight project workspace. Code, configuration, notebooks, templates, and documentation remain in the user workspace; the data/ directory points to persistent external storage on the mounted Hyperdisk.

title: Migration, Upgrade, and Recovery
text:
Optional: Data Preservation Snapshot






Note



Required only when rebuilding, migrating, or cloning an existing VM rather than deploying from a fresh image — for example, during machine family changes or environment rebuilds where the existing data and configuration need to be preserved.


Before creating a snapshot, stop any active services writing to the disk to ensure filesystem consistency:
sudo systemctl stop tomcat
Create a persistent disk snapshot from Cloud Shell or another authenticated terminal:
gcloud compute disks snapshot YOUR_INSTANCE_NAME \
    --project=YOUR_GCP_PROJECT_ID \
    --zone=YOUR_GCP_ZONE \
    --snapshot-names=YOUR_INSTANCE_NAME-migration-backup


Optional: Migration Snapshot Cleanup






Note



Required only when rebuilding or migrating an existing VM using temporary manual snapshots. In many environments, temporary migration snapshots are retained for at least 24 hours before cleanup.


After the new environment has been validated and operating normally, delete the migration snapshot:
gcloud compute snapshots delete YOUR_INSTANCE_NAME-migration-backup --quiet

title: GCP Data Processing Templates
text:
Template Repositories
To support consistent, reproducible workflows across projects, SWFSC CoastWatch maintains two GitHub template repositories. These templates provide a standardized starting point for new data processing projects deployed on Google Cloud Platform.

title: GCP Data Processing Templates
text:
Cloud Run Template
Repository: erd-coastwatch/cloud-run-template
Use this template for containerized batch processing jobs deployed on Google Cloud Run. It follows the same structure used by the VIIRS NetPP, MUR SST, and CHARM workflows and is designed for jobs that run on a schedule or on demand, process data, and write results to GCS.
cloud-run-template/
├── config/
│   ├── config.yml          # Runtime settings (sensors, GCS paths, API keys)
│   └── requirements.txt    # Python dependencies
├── deploy_job.sh           # Build, push, and deploy the Cloud Run job
├── Dockerfile              # Container definition
├── entrypoint.sh           # Job mode selector
├── scripts/                # Processing and orchestration logic
└── templates/              # CDL or other output templates


Dataproc / VM Template
Repository: erd-coastwatch/vm-dataproc-template
Use this template for interactive or long-running workflows that run directly on a Dataproc or processing VM. It is structured for projects that combine notebooks, scripts, and reusable Python modules, with access to GCS-mounted data.
vm-dataproc-template/
├── README.md               # Project overview and setup instructions
├── environment.yml         # Conda environment
├── config.yml              # Configuration file
├── LICENSE
├── notebooks/              # Jupyter notebooks for exploration and analysis
├── scripts/                # Shell and Python workflow scripts
├── src/                    # Reusable Python modules and helpers
├── data/                   # Symlink to persistent storage on the mounted processing disk
├── templates/              # CDL or CSV template files
├── resources/              # Misc reference files used during processing
├── logs/                   # Runtime logs
├── docs/                   # Documentation (Quarto or Sphinx)
└── tests/                  # Unit tests
GCS mount points follow the convention:
/mnt/gcs/data/<project>/           # Large input data
/mnt/gcs/erddap_data_pw/<project>/ # ERDDAP-served output data
The data/ directory in the template is a symlink to project-specific persistent storage on the mounted Hyperdisk, keeping large datasets and workflow outputs separated from the VM boot disk. See the Processing VM Setup page for the full workspace initialization steps.
Periodically review the template repository for updates to the standard project structure.

title: GCP Data Processing Templates
text:
How to Use a Template

Go to the template repo on GitHub and click “Use this template” → “Create a new repository”.
Name your repo following the project naming convention (e.g. viirs-netpp, seaice-sst).
Clone your new repo to your local machine or VM:

git clone https://github.com/erd-coastwatch/YOUR_NEW_REPO.git
cd YOUR_NEW_REPO

Fill in config.yml with your project-specific settings — sensor names, GCS bucket paths, date ranges, and any API parameters.
Add your scripts and notebooks to the appropriate directories.
For Cloud Run projects, build and deploy using deploy_job.sh once your Dockerfile and scripts are ready.







Note



These templates are intentionally minimal — they provide structure, not implementation. Each project fills in the logic specific to its data source and processing requirements.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow used to retrieve and publish MODIS-Aqua Level-3 satellite products for the MH1, MPOC, and MPIC datsets served on ERDDAP. The workflow handles both near-real-time (NRT) and science-quality streams for ocean color, sea surface temperature, particulate organic carbon, and particulate inorganic carbon products.
The workflow replaces legacy server-based retrieval scripts with a containerized cloud workflow. It queries the NASA OceanColor file search API, compares available files against products already staged in Google Cloud Storage (GCS), downloads only missing files, publishes final products to ERDDAP-facing storage paths, and removes temporary files from the Cloud Run runtime.
The processor manages three related MODIS-Aqua product families:
Each product stream supports 1-day, 8-day, and monthly composites where available. NRT and science-quality products are handled separately so that ERDDAP can expose both recent operational products and delayed higher-quality products.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
MH1 Ocean Color — chlorophyll-a, nFLH, PAR, and Kd490
MH1 SST — raw sea surface temperature plus a masked SST variant that retains pixels where qual_sst >= 0
MPOC — particulate organic carbon
MPIC — particulate inorganic carbon

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Repository structure
mh1-mpoc-mpic-processor/
├── README.md                   # Operational overview and deployment notes
├── config/
│   ├── config.yml              # Runtime settings, GCS targets, NASA endpoints
│   └── requirements.txt        # Python dependencies
├── deploy_job.sh               # Build, push, deploy, and schedule the Cloud Run job
├── Dockerfile                  # Container definition
├── entrypoint.sh               # Runs all NRT suites first, then all science-quality suites
├── scripts/
│   ├── MH1/
│   │   ├── getMH1OceanColor_NRT.py   # Ocean color NRT retrieval
│   │   ├── getMH1OceanColor_Sci.py   # Ocean color science-quality retrieval
│   │   ├── getMH1SST_NRT.py          # SST NRT retrieval and masking
│   │   └── getMH1SST_Sci.py          # SST science-quality retrieval and masking
│   ├── MPIC/
│   │   ├── getMPIC_NRT.py            # PIC NRT retrieval
│   │   └── getMPIC_Sci.py            # PIC science-quality retrieval
│   └── MPOC/
│       ├── getMPOC_NRT.py            # POC NRT retrieval
│       └── getMPOC_Sci.py            # POC science-quality retrieval
└── src/
    ├── __init__.py
    └── roylib.py                     # Shared configuration, GCS, NASA, and publishing utilities

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Relationship to the workflow repository
The source workflow repository includes a README.md with the operational overview, local development notes, deployment requirements, and runtime expectations. This Quarto chapter provides the public-facing documentation view of the same workflow.
The copy shown in this book is a sanitized documentation snapshot. Before rendering, real bucket names, project IDs, internal hostnames, local filesystem paths, email addresses, credential files, and deployment-specific values are removed or replaced with generic placeholders.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Cloud Run job
The workflow runs as a scheduled Cloud Run job. The job entrypoint prepares writable runtime directories under /tmp, copies Earthdata credential files from Secret Manager mounts, and then runs the retrieval suites in sequence: NRT products first, followed by science-quality products.








Suite
Scripts
Description




MH1 NRT
getMH1OceanColor_NRT.py, getMH1SST_NRT.py
Ocean color and SST near-real-time retrieval


MH1 Science
getMH1OceanColor_Sci.py, getMH1SST_Sci.py
Ocean color and SST science-quality retrieval


MPIC NRT
getMPIC_NRT.py
PIC near-real-time retrieval


MPIC Science
getMPIC_Sci.py
PIC science-quality retrieval


MPOC NRT
getMPOC_NRT.py
POC near-real-time retrieval


MPOC Science
getMPOC_Sci.py
POC science-quality retrieval

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Processing pipeline
Each retrieval suite follows the same operational pattern:
Cloud Scheduler
  → Cloud Run Job
  → Query NASA OceanColor file search API
  → Compare available NASA files against existing GCS objects
  → Download missing files to /tmp
  → Publish outputs to configured GCS destinations
  → Remove local temporary files
The NASA OceanColor file_search API is queried for the current calendar year. Returned filenames are compared against the production bucket inventory from list_bucket_content(). Only files absent from GCS are downloaded using Earthdata credentials provided at runtime. This keeps the job incremental and avoids repeatedly downloading products that have already been staged.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Runtime configuration
Runtime settings are loaded from config/config.yml through roylib.py. In Cloud Run, the config file is mounted from Secret Manager and the path is provided through the ROYLIB_CONFIG environment variable.
The workflow distinguishes between two major GCS destinations:







Setting
Purpose




ERDPROD_BUCKET
Production bucket for final ERDDAP-facing NetCDF products


ERDWORK_BUCKET
Work/staging bucket for intermediate, mirrored, or operational support files



The standard publishing logic is implemented in send_to_servers():

Every output is uploaded to the production bucket.
Selected 1-day MB/MW outputs are also copied to the work bucket using the legacy regional layout.

Example destination patterns:
gs://<prod_bucket>/<prod_root>/<dst_dir>/<interval>day/<filename>
gs://<work_bucket>/<work_root>/<region>/1day/<filename>
Bucket values in config.yml are plain bucket names, not gs:// URIs.
The configuration also provides:

NASA OceanColor API endpoints
ERDDAP-facing publish destinations
local runtime paths used inside the container
Earthdata credential file locations
product-specific destination mappings

Some configuration files may contain settings shared with other CoastWatch workflows. The MH1 processor uses the subset related to MODIS-Aqua MH1, MPOC, MPIC, NASA file search, and GCS publishing.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
NASA API dataset IDs
The scripts query NASA OceanColor using dataset identifiers (dtid) for MODIS-Aqua Level-3 mapped products. The configured queries use sensor_id=7, corresponding to MODIS-Aqua.








Dataset
dtid
Notes




MH1 Ocean Color NRT
1055
MODIS-Aqua Level-3 mapped NRT ocean color products


MH1 Ocean Color Science
1043
MODIS-Aqua Level-3 mapped science-quality ocean color products


MH1 SST NRT
1061
MODIS-Aqua Level-3 mapped NRT SST products


MH1 SST Science
1049
MODIS-Aqua Level-3 mapped science-quality SST products


MPOC / MPIC NRT
1055
MODIS-Aqua Level-3 mapped NRT ocean color stream used for carbon products


MPOC / MPIC Science
1102
MODIS-Aqua Level-3 mapped science-quality carbon products

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
GCS path conventions
NRT products are published under satellite/<DATASET>_NRT/... paths, while science-quality products are published under satellite/<DATASET>/... paths. The exact product and period subdirectories vary by stream, but follow the same naming pattern used by the legacy ERDDAP-facing archive.
Example object prefixes:
satellite/MH1_NRT/chla/1day/
satellite/MH1/chla/1day/
satellite/MH1_NRT/sst/mday/
satellite/MH1/sstMask/8day/
satellite/MPOC_NRT/1day/
satellite/MPIC/mday/

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
SST masking
For both NRT and science-quality SST products, the workflow generates a masked SST file alongside the raw NASA download. The masking step reads the raw sst variable and the qual_sst quality variable, then sets SST values to fill where qual_sst < 0.
Pixels where qual_sst >= 0 are retained and written to an output variable named sstMasked. Raw SST files and masked SST files are published to separate configured GCS paths so ERDDAP can expose both the original NASA SST product and the CoastWatch masked variant.

title: MH1 / MPOC / MPIC Processor
text:
Credentials and secrets
Earthdata Login credentials are provided to the Cloud Run job through Google Secret Manager. The job expects .netrc and URS cookie files to be available from Secret Manager mounts at runtime. The entrypoint copies these files into writable /tmp locations before the Python retrieval scripts run.
Expected runtime credential paths include:
/tmp/.netrc
/tmp/.urs_cookies
These files are runtime credentials only. They should not be committed to the source repository or included in the public Quarto documentation copy.

title: MODIS-Aqua Primary Productivity
text:
Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow for generating MODIS-Aqua MH1 net primary productivity products for ERDDAP publication.
The workflow uses MODIS-Aqua chlorophyll-a, PAR, SST, and daylength inputs to compute satellite-derived primary productivity. It is containerized for Cloud Run Jobs and publishes final NetCDF products to cloud storage for ERDDAP ingestion.
The MH1 primary productivity workflow extends the MODIS-Aqua MH1 product family by generating daily net primary productivity outputs from existing ocean-color, SST, and light-field inputs.
The workflow is designed to:

title: MODIS-Aqua Primary Productivity
text:
run operationally as a scheduled Cloud Run Job
process recent daily dates in near-real time
support backfill processing over historical date ranges
write ERDDAP-facing NetCDF outputs
publish completed products to cloud storage
keep large static inputs, such as daylength arrays, out of Git

title: MODIS-Aqua Primary Productivity
text:
Repository structure
mh1-primprod/
├── config/
│   ├── config.yml          # Runtime configuration
│   └── requirements.txt    # Python dependencies
├── deploy_job.sh           # Build, deploy, and schedule Cloud Run job
├── Dockerfile              # Container definition
├── entrypoint.sh           # Container startup script
├── scripts/
│   └── control_mh1_primprod.py
├── src/
│   ├── npp_utils.py
│   ├── primprodUtil.py
│   ├── ppCompositeUtil.py
│   └── __init__.py
└── templates/
    └── daylen.nc           # Large static daylength file; excluded from public code rendering

title: MODIS-Aqua Primary Productivity
text:
Processing model
The workflow computes primary productivity from daily satellite inputs. The active Cloud Run controller coordinates date selection, input discovery, processing, and publication.
At a high level, each run follows this sequence:
Cloud Scheduler
  -> Cloud Run Job
  -> entrypoint.sh
  -> scripts/control_mh1_primprod.py
  -> src/npp_utils.py
  -> src/primprodUtil.py
  -> NetCDF output
  -> cloud publication
  -> ERDDAP ingestion
The productivity calculation uses the legacy primary productivity utilities retained in src/primprodUtil.py, while Cloud Run-specific orchestration, download, staging, and publishing behavior is handled by src/npp_utils.py.

title: MODIS-Aqua Primary Productivity
text:
Runtime behavior
The workflow supports operational recent-date runs and historical backfills.
Typical processing behavior includes:

Resolve the requested processing date or date range.
Locate required MODIS-Aqua input products.
Stage inputs in the container runtime filesystem.
Load the static daylength data.
Compute primary productivity.
Write the output NetCDF product.
Compress or finalize the NetCDF output as configured.
Publish the final product to cloud storage.
Clean temporary files.

The container uses /tmp for writable runtime work because Cloud Run containers should not rely on persistent local state.

text:
Runtime configuration
Runtime settings are defined in config/config.yml and supplemented by environment variables set at deployment time.

Publication bucket and prefixes for ERDDAP-facing products.

Local scratch, template, and working directories.


Processing dates
Recent-run and backfill date selection.


Static assets
Daylength input file expected by the productivity model.



The public documentation copy shown here is sanitized. Deployment-specific values such as bucket names, project IDs, service accounts, private paths, and credentials are shown with generic placeholders.

The workflow depends on a static daylength NetCDF file:
templates/daylen.nc
This file is large and should not be committed to the Quarto documentation repository. The workflow documentation notes its required role, but the binary file is intentionally excluded from rendered code pages and from the sanitized public workflow copy.
If the workflow is deployed or rebuilt, the daylength file should be supplied through the operational repository, a controlled artifact, or cloud storage rather than embedded in the public documentation site.

title: MUR SST
text:
Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow used to retrieve, process, and publish Multi-scale Ultra-high Resolution (MUR) Sea Surface Temperature (SST) products for ERDDAP-facing datasets.
The workflow replaces legacy server-based processing with a containerized cloud workflow. It stages MUR data from NASA PO.DAAC, detects when available products have been updated from near-real-time to final quality, generates derived MUR v4.1 products, and publishes outputs to configured Google Cloud Storage (GCS) paths used by ERDDAP.
The MUR SST workflow supports two related product streams:
MUR v4.1 is the primary processing stream for derived products. MUR v4.2 is handled as a daily synchronization stream so the archive can track availability and quality upgrades.

title: MUR SST
text:
MUR v4.1 — daily SST retrieval, daily anomaly generation, frontal edge detection, monthly SST composites, and monthly anomaly composites
MUR v4.2 — daily SST retrieval and smart synchronization

title: MUR SST
text:
Repository structure
mur-daily-workflows/
├── README.md                       # Operational overview and deployment notes
├── config/
│   ├── config.yml                  # Runtime settings, GCS targets, PO.DAAC collections
│   └── requirements.txt            # Python dependencies
├── deploy_job.sh                   # Build, push, deploy, and schedule Cloud Run jobs
├── Dockerfile                      # Container definition
├── entrypoint.sh                   # Job mode selector
├── scripts/
│   ├── MURanom1day.py              # Daily MUR v4.1 anomaly computation
│   ├── mur_v41_downloader_dailyproc.sh
│   │                               # MUR v4.1 daily retrieval, anomaly, and fronts driver
│   ├── mur_v42_downloader.sh       # MUR v4.2 daily retrieval driver
│   ├── fronts/
│   │   ├── calc_mur_fronts.py      # Regional fronts runner
│   │   ├── canny_lib.py            # Front-detection utilities
│   │   ├── Canny1.py               # West Coast Canny implementation
│   │   └── Canny2.py               # Atlantic Canny implementation
│   └── MonthlyProc/
│       ├── MUR41_MonProc.sh        # Monthly processing driver
│       ├── CompMURmon.py           # Monthly SST composite generation
│       └── CompMurAnomMon.py       # Monthly anomaly composite generation
├── src/
│   └── roylib.py                   # Workflow-specific config and GCS publishing utilities
└── templates/
    ├── murAnomTemplate.nc          # Template for daily anomaly output
    ├── murSSTmday.nc               # Template for monthly SST composite
    └── murSSTmdayAnom.nc           # Template for monthly anomaly composite

title: MUR SST
text:
Cloud Run jobs
The workflow uses a single container image with multiple Cloud Run job modes. The entrypoint.sh script dispatches to the appropriate processing script based on the JOB_MODE environment variable.








Job mode
Script
Description




mur41_daily
mur_v41_downloader_dailyproc.sh
MUR v4.1 daily retrieval, anomaly generation, and fronts processing


mur42_daily
mur_v42_downloader.sh
MUR v4.2 daily retrieval and quality-aware synchronization


mur41_monthly
MUR41_MonProc.sh
MUR v4.1 monthly SST and anomaly composites



This separation allows daily and monthly products to run on different schedules while using the same container, configuration file, and deployment pattern.

title: MUR SST
text:
Processing pipeline
The daily MUR v4.1 workflow follows this pattern:
Cloud Scheduler
  → Cloud Run Job
  → Check existing GCS product
  → Check NASA PO.DAAC / CMR availability
  → Download or refresh source file when needed
  → Publish daily SST
  → Generate and publish daily SST anomaly
  → Generate and publish regional frontal products
  → Remove local temporary files
The MUR v4.2 workflow follows the same synchronization pattern but does not generate anomaly or frontal products.

title: MUR SST
text:
Quality-aware synchronization
The daily download scripts check both the product already staged in GCS and the product currently available from NASA. This allows the workflow to detect when a near-real-time granule has been replaced or promoted to a final-quality product.
If the existing GCS copy is still near-real-time and NASA now provides the final version, the workflow re-downloads the file and republishes the updated product. This keeps the ERDDAP-facing archive current without requiring full reprocessing of the entire record.

title: MUR SST
text:
Runtime configuration
Runtime settings are loaded from config/config.yml. The configuration defines:

PO.DAAC collection names
production bucket and publish root
MUR v4.1 and v4.2 destination directories
daily and monthly interval conventions
local scratch directories used inside the Cloud Run container
the MUR climatology location used for anomaly processing
optional ERDDAP reload or flag settings

Bucket-name fields are plain bucket names, not gs:// URIs. Full GCS URI settings are only used where the value represents a complete input location, such as a climatology prefix.
Example destination pattern:
gs://<prod_bucket>/<prod_root>/<prod_dst_dir>/<interval>day/<filename>

title: MUR SST
text:
MUR v4.1 daily products
The MUR v4.1 daily job retrieves daily SST products and publishes them to the configured production path. After the SST product is staged, the workflow runs the daily anomaly script and the regional front-detection scripts.
Daily MUR v4.1 outputs include:

daily SST
daily SST anomaly
West Coast frontal edge product
Atlantic frontal edge product

The daily SST anomaly is computed relative to the configured MUR climatology. The anomaly output is written using the NetCDF template in templates/murAnomTemplate.nc.

title: MUR SST
text:
Frontal edge products
Frontal products are generated from MUR v4.1 SST using Canny edge detection. The workflow currently processes two regional subsets:



Region
Code
Approximate coverage




U.S. West Coast
WC
22°N–51°N, 135°W–105°W


U.S. Atlantic Coast
ATL
20°N–50°N, 90°W–60°W



The front-detection output includes frontal edge masks, x/y gradients, and gradient magnitude fields. The workflow uses calc_mur_fronts.py as the regional runner and canny_lib.py as the shared front-processing utility layer.

title: MUR SST
text:
Monthly composites
Monthly processing is triggered with JOB_MODE=mur41_monthly. The monthly driver runs two compositors:

CompMURmon.py — builds a monthly mean SST composite from daily MUR v4.1 SST files.
CompMurAnomMon.py — builds a monthly mean anomaly composite from daily anomaly files.

The target month is controlled by the MONTH_OFFSET environment variable. The default behavior is to process the last completed month.
The monthly compositors download the relevant daily files from GCS, process them in parallel where appropriate, and write outputs using the monthly NetCDF templates in templates/.

title: MUR SST
text:
Publishing model
Final products are uploaded to the configured production bucket under the configured root prefix. Product-specific destination directories and interval labels are defined in config/config.yml.
Example output families include:
MUR41/ssta/1day/
MUR41/anom/1day/
MUR41/erdMurFront41USWest/1day/
MUR41/erdMurFront41USAtlantic/1day/
MUR41/ssta/mday/
MUR41/anom/mday/
MUR42/ssta/1day/
The simplified src/roylib.py used by this workflow provides the shared configuration loader and GCS publishing helper used by the monthly processing scripts.

title: MUR SST
text:
Credentials and runtime files
NASA access and Google Cloud access are handled at runtime. The container uses Cloud Run service account permissions for GCS access, while NASA/Earthdata credentials are provided through the runtime environment.
Credential files such as .netrc and URS cookie files are runtime-only files and are not part of the public documentation copy.

Codebase

title: CRW SST/SSTA
text:
Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow for generating NOAA Coral Reef Watch (CRW) 5 km sea surface temperature (SST) and sea surface temperature anomaly (SSTA) products for ERDDAP publication.
The workflow downloads daily and monthly CRW NetCDF source products, combines SST and SSTA fields into ERDDAP-facing NetCDF outputs, compresses the results, archives source files, and publishes final products to cloud storage.
The CRW workflow maintains combined SST/SSTA products derived from NOAA Coral Reef Watch source files. These products support ERDDAP access to daily and monthly CRW temperature fields using a consistent output structure and file naming pattern.
The workflow produces two main product streams:
Raw source files are archived separately under a source-data prefix so the final merged products can be regenerated if needed.

title: CRW SST/SSTA
text:
Product stream
Output prefix
Output pattern




Daily CRW SST/SSTA
satellite/CRW2/1day/
ct5km_sst_ssta_daily_v31_YYYYMMDD.nc


Monthly CRW SST/SSTA
satellite/CRW2/mday/

title: CRW SST/SSTA
text:
Cloud Run jobs
The same container image is used for both daily and monthly processing. The JOB_TYPE environment variable controls which controller is run by entrypoint.sh.








Job type
Controller
Description




daily
control_crw_daily.py
Checks for missing daily CRW SST/SSTA outputs and builds recent missing days.


monthly
control_crw_monthly.py
Checks for missing monthly CRW SST/SSTA outputs over a rolling two-year window.



The deployment script creates two Cloud Run Jobs:








Cloud Run Job
Runtime mode
Schedule

JOB_TYPE=daily
Daily at 10:00 America/Los_Angeles


crw-monthly-processor
JOB_TYPE=monthly
04:00 America/Los_Angeles on days 2, 4, 10, 12, 15, and 18 of each month



The monthly schedule mirrors the legacy retry cadence and gives upstream monthly CRW files multiple chances to appear before the workflow stops checking.

title: CRW SST/SSTA
text:
Daily processing
The daily controller evaluates a short lookback window, skips dates already present in cloud storage, and calls the daily worker only for missing final outputs.
By default, daily processing checks the last three days, excluding today. For backfills, BACKFILL_START_DATE=YYYY-MM-DD can be set to evaluate all dates from that start date through yesterday while still skipping outputs that already exist.
For each daily date, the worker:

Downloads the daily CRW SST file.
Downloads the daily CRW SSTA file.
Archives both source files to the work/source bucket.
Copies the static latitude/longitude template into a temporary output file.
Reads SST, SSTA, and mask variables from the source files.
Aligns SST latitude orientation to the output template.
Writes SST, SSTA, mask, and time values into the merged NetCDF output.
Compresses the merged output with nccopy.
Uploads the final daily product to cloud storage.
Removes temporary local files.

title: CRW SST/SSTA
text:
Monthly processing
The monthly controller evaluates a rolling two-year monthly window through the previous completed month. It checks cloud storage for existing monthly products and only builds missing months.
For each missing month, the worker:

Downloads the monthly CRW SST mean file.
Downloads the monthly CRW SSTA mean file.
Archives both source files to the work/source bucket.
Copies the static latitude/longitude template into a temporary output file.
Writes monthly SST, SSTA, mask, and time values into the merged NetCDF output.
Compresses the merged output with nccopy.
Uploads the final monthly product to cloud storage.
Removes temporary local files.

title: CRW SST/SSTA
text:
Runtime configuration
The workflow is currently configured mostly through environment variables and script-level constants.








Variable
Default
Purpose




GCS_BUCKET
production bucket
Target bucket for final ERDDAP-facing outputs


JOB_TYPE
monthly
Selects daily or monthly processing


LOOKBACK_DAYS
3
Number of recent days evaluated by the daily controller


BACKFILL_START_DATE
unset
Optional daily backfill start date in YYYY-MM-DD format



The current config/config.yml file is reserved for future configuration centralization. Unlike some of the other workflows in this book, this workflow still keeps several source URLs, output prefixes, and archive settings directly in the scripts.

Static template
The workflow uses templates/lat_lon_source2023b.nc as the static output template/grid source for the merged products.
Because this is a binary NetCDF file, it is included in the workflow repository but is not rendered as a Quarto code page.

title: ASCAT-C Wind Products
text:
Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow for generating CoastWatch-style ASCAT-C wind products for ERDDAP publication.
The workflow ingests Metop-C ASCAT 4-hour wind files, derives value-added wind products, writes CoastWatch-compatible NetCDF outputs, and builds temporal composites from those 4-hour products.
The ASCAT-C workflow supports operational wind-product generation from scatterometer observations. It produces both native 4-hour products and higher-level temporal composites.
The workflow produces:
Derived fields include zonal and meridional wind components, wind stress, wind curl, wind divergence, wind-driven currents, and Ekman upwelling.

title: ASCAT-C Wind Products
text:
Product family
Description




4-hour products
Value-added ASCAT-C wind products derived from source 4-hour wind files.


1-day composites
Daily composites built from 4-hour products.


3-day composites
Rolling 3-day composites built from 4-hour products.


7-day composites
Rolling 7-day composites built from 4-hour products.


Monthly composites
Monthly means built from daily ASCAT-C composites.

The same container image supports daily and monthly processing. Runtime behavior is selected by the JOB_MODE environment variable in entrypoint.sh.







Mode
Description

monthly

The deployment script creates two Cloud Run Jobs:

Runtime mode

22:30 America/Los_Angeles on the 3rd of each month

title: ASCAT-C Wind Products
text:
Daily workflow
The daily job performs two stages.
First, the container warms its local /tmp cache by copying recent 4-hour ASCAT-C outputs from cloud storage. This gives the composite step enough input files to build 1-, 3-, and 7-day products even though Cloud Run starts with an empty filesystem.
Second, the workflow runs the 4-hour ingestion and composite drivers:
entrypoint.sh
  -> scripts.download_ascat_4hr
  -> scripts.ascat_composite_control multi-day
  -> scripts.make_ascat_multiday
The 4-hour ingestion step:

Scrapes the remote NOAA ASCAT-C source directory.
Compares remote files to already-published 4-hour files in cloud storage.
Downloads only missing source files.
Computes derived wind products.
Writes CoastWatch-compatible NetCDF outputs from the configured template.
Uploads final 4-hour outputs to cloud storage.
Cleans local staging files.

The multi-day composite step:

Selects the target date, usually yesterday in UTC.
Finds the required 4-hour inputs for each composite window.
Uses NCO tools to concatenate and average input files.
Applies time and global metadata.
Compresses the final NetCDF products.
Publishes 1-, 3-, and 7-day outputs to cloud storage.

title: ASCAT-C Wind Products
text:
Monthly workflow
The monthly job builds monthly ASCAT-C composites from daily products.
At startup, the container warms the previous month of daily files from cloud storage. It then runs:
entrypoint.sh
  -> scripts.ascat_composite_control monthly
  -> scripts.make_ascat_multiday --make-month
The monthly composite step:

Selects the previous completed month.
Finds the daily files for that month.
Uses NCO tools to average the daily inputs.
Sets the monthly center time to the 16th day of the month.
Compresses the monthly output.
Publishes the monthly product to cloud storage.

title: NSIDC Sea Ice
text:
Purpose
This chapter documents the Cloud Run workflow for processing NSIDC G02202 v6 sea ice concentration products for ERDDAP publication.
The workflow downloads or stages NSIDC source data, prepares daily sea ice files, builds monthly composites, and publishes ERDDAP-facing NetCDF outputs to cloud storage.
The NSIDC workflow supports operational publication of sea ice concentration products from the NSIDC Climate Data Record product family.
The workflow is designed to:

process daily NSIDC G02202 v6 sea ice concentration files
build monthly composites from daily products
support scheduled Cloud Run execution
publish outputs to cloud storage for ERDDAP ingestion
keep the daily and monthly workflow logic in a single containerized repository

title: NSIDC Sea Ice

Daily workflow
The daily workflow prepares ERDDAP-facing NSIDC sea ice concentration files from daily source data.
A typical daily run follows this sequence:

Resolve the requested processing date or recent-date window.
Locate the corresponding NSIDC G02202 v6 daily source file.
Stage the source file in the container runtime filesystem.
Prepare the output NetCDF file and metadata.
Publish the final daily product to cloud storage.

Daily products are organized by hemisphere, date, and year so ERDDAP can scan stable cloud-backed directory structures.

A typical monthly run follows this sequence:

Combine daily fields into a monthly product.
Write monthly metadata and time coverage values.
Publish the final monthly composite to cloud storage.
